dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for Falls at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi ma'am, I'm Jordan, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I'm Taylor, an EMT. We're here to help you. Can you tell us your name?
Her name is Mary. She fell this morning and has a large cut on her head, bleeding for at least an hour. We can't control the blood.
Okay, thank you. Are you experiencing any pain or dizziness?
Ow! My head! I think it's bad! Jesus take me, they're hurting me!
Can you tell me your name, Mary?
Mary, yes. Mary Thompson.
Mary, what day is today?
...I don't know. Is it a holiday?
That's okay. Mary, do you know where you are?
I was... I was in the bathroom... I fell.
She opens her eyes to speech.
She responds to verbal cues with words but is disoriented.
She follows directional commands like reaching for a towel, but movements are slow.
Ma'am, can you maintain your own airway?
It appears the airway is patent and unobstructed at this time.
You're not short of breath. Let me check your respiration.
Respiratory rate: 20, breath sounds clear bilaterally.
Pulse oximetry is at 97%, no cyanosis noted at this time.
Respiratory rate is now 12 with a regular pattern.
She feels warm and dry. Let me check your pulse.
Pulse rate is 97, strong and regular with a regular rhythm.
Blood pressure is 128 over 84. No evidence of JVD noted at this time.
Oxygen saturation is 97% with normal breathing effort.
Blood sugar reading is 0; we'll need to continue monitoring this value.
No signs of tachycardia. Heart rhythm is sinus on EKG. No distress observed at present.
When did the fall happen?
We're not certain, but it was sometime before housekeeping called. She was found on the bathroom floor with a bleeding head injury.
Does she have any known medical conditions?
Yes, she has hypertension, dementia, and a history of epilepsy.
She is 85 years old, female, no known allergies, current medications include Keppra.
When did the head pain start?
It must have started when she fell, but she can't remember exactly.
Does moving your head make the pain worse?
Yes, it screams and throbs when I touch!
Can you describe the pain? Sharp, stabbing?
It's terrible! Throbbing, like a hammer hitting.
On a scale of 1 to 10, with 10 being the worst possible, how bad is your pain?
She says it is a 10.
Does the pain go anywhere else?
No... stays right here, in the back.
This has been going on since the fall. Is that right?
We're checking your pupils. They are equal and reactive to light, PERRLA.
Okay?
Pupils are equal and reactive. Trachea is midline. No JVD noted now or previously.
There is a significant bleed soaking the top of your clothing. A 5 cm laceration to the left occipital scalp is present with active bleeding.
There are bloody footprints on the floor and a pool of blood in the bathroom. Evidence suggests this injury occurred before our arrival.
Your lungs are clear and symmetrical, breath sounds present bilaterally. No work of breathing observed with good chest expansion on both sides.
Your belly is soft and tender on palpation, no distension. It is non-guarded and without rigidity at this moment.
You have full movement in your arms and legs. Our assessment will include motor and sensory components next for full exam.
Blood pressure is 115 over 78 with no change in JVD status noted.
Now 117 over 69. No peripheral cyanosis or JVD remains observed at present time.
Her heart rate is 88, then 81. Stable with no irregularity or change in rhythm currently observed.
SpO₂ is 99%, then 98%. No issues in peripheral oxygen delivery so far.
Respiratory rate is steady at 12, no signs of distress since intervention started but remains closely observed.
Ma'am, we're placing a trauma dressing on the head wound. It will feel tight on your skull area. This is a necessity to try and control the bleeding as best we can under current circumstances.
Trauma dressing applied but bleeding remains apparent with no significant reduction in active bleed.
Added pressure dressing, but no complete control. Still actively bleeding. We monitor constantly as wound shows no signs of slowing with pressure alone.
We're starting an IV in your arm to give normal saline and fluids for hydration and support pressure maintaining blood volume during transport preparations.
IV is up, running normal saline at 500 mL. Monitor is in place for any immediate change in response or need for further bolus fluid administration if necessary during transport.
Applying 4-lead EKG monitoring to record your cardiac rhythm and detect any changes. We will continuously watch this throughout the response.
We are operating under Hemorrhage Control Protocol 4-14. This will guide us as we continue with vital signs and monitoring efforts throughout.
Ma'am, we are prepared to transport you to Henrico Doctors' Hospital for immediate assessments and treatments given your current condition.
We'll now carefully move you to the stretcher with a draw sheet and into semi-Fowler's position. This position is preferred for your comfort during transport.
Securing you with seat belts to ensure a safe transport. We plan to use five seat belts for maximum stability during transfer.
Vital signs show pulse 81, respirations 12, BP at 117 over 69. All readings remain stable per protocol.
Your pain remains a 10, correct?
Yes! All I feel is pain in the back of my skull!
SpO₂ is 98%, EKG remains sinus rhythm. Glucose remains at 0. All signs remain stable.
Patient is in care of Hospice. Initially advised to wait, but the family has now consented for transport. We received verbal consent from daughter/POA on scene.
Our interventions remain unchanged, IV fluids and trauma dressing are in place. No additional interventions are needed at this time.
We had delays convincing the family. They now agree to transport. Please remain calm.
Be advised, delays may occur at the hospital for decontamination. Measures will be taken to ensure safety during this process.
Report has been given to Tim Kennedy, RN. Full patient rundown is now in their hands at the hospital.
You are secured on the stretcher. We will keep you comfortable during transport.
Patient is experiencing profound head pain. We're transporting her for neurological and imaging assessment at the hospital.
Vital signs trending down but no signs of shock or hypoxia. Condition remains stable for now.
Patient is being moved from the ambulance to the emergency room on a trauma bed via draw sheet. Ensuring the smoothest possible transition to hospital services.
Transport initiated at safe speed to the hospital. We'll maintain continuous monitoring and reassess at regular intervals throughout.
Initially noted cyanotic changes during assessment but resolved to warm and dry. No persistent JVD or cyanosis is present now.
Pelvis is stable, no signs of instability or tenderness on palpation.
Motor function is intact in all extremities. We'll assess sensation next as part of neurological status check.
We made multiple attempts to stop the bleeding on scene. No successful intervention at this time, so fluid support remains critical.
